nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef slopes, lagoons, and turbid inshore zones; typically found on rock, rubble, and dead coral substrate in lower-light, lower-flow positions

decorPreferences: live rock or rubble base for attachment
lower rockwork placement away from direct flow
spacing from neighboring corals to prevent chemical aggression

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Benefits from supplemental target-feeding of small meaty foods such as brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or microplankton approximately once per week per polyp. Spot feeding by pipette near the central mouth tends to improve growth rate and coloration. Tolerates slightly nutrient-rich water better than most corals.

goodWith: clownfish (may be used as host anemone substitute)
other mushroom corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals placed in direct contact (torch, hammer, frogspawn, chalice)
large urchins that may dislodge specimens
digging or substrate-disturbing fish near placement

summary: Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral is a beginner-friendly corallimorpharian widely sold under its trade name though currently synonymized as Discosoma. Hardy, fast-reproducing, and tolerant; thrives under low-to-moderate light and low flow.
careNotes:
Taxonomic note: Actinodiscus de Blainville, 1830 is widely treated as a junior synonym of Discosoma Rüppell & Leuckart, 1828 in current scientific literature (confirmed by Wikipedia; family Discosomidae, order Corallimorpharia). Despite this, 'Actinodiscus' remains the dominant trade name used by aquarium retailers (LiveAquaria, World Wide Corals) and in aquarium hobby communication. This entry uses 'Actinodiscus sp.' as scientificName to preserve the trade name; the synonymy with Discosoma sp. is documented here and in alsoKnownAs.

This entry covers the broad genus Actinodiscus (= Discosoma) as sold in the trade, encompassing multiple species with near-identical husbandry requirements. Color variants and trade morphs (solid, spotted, striped, iridescent, metallic) are sold under different common names but are generally conspecific or congenerics with identical care.

Lighting: Low to moderate intensity is generally advised, approximately 40–100 PAR for most Discosoma species. Some sources suggest up to 150 PAR is tolerated, but the consensus for stable captive keeping is the 50–100 PAR sweet spot. Bleaching under intense light is commonly reported; start specimens low and acclimate upward slowly.

Flow: Low flow is strongly preferred. Direct powerhead current has been known to cause shrinking, tissue stress, and detachment. Indirect or alternating gentle flow is ideal; the polyps should sit relaxed and open, not compressed or folded.

Placement: Lower tank zones are recommended where light and flow are gentler. Multiple specimens can be placed together in a colony arrangement, but individual polyps spread via asexual reproduction and can colonize available substrate rapidly.

Aggression: Semi-aggressive — has been known to sting and overgrow neighboring corals via allelopathic chemical release when colonizing in large numbers. Maintaining spacing from other coral types is generally advised. Running activated carbon is sometimes recommended in dense mushroom tanks to mitigate allelopathy.

Temperature source spread: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); ReefChasers lists 77–84°F (25.0–28.9°C). These ranges partially overlap and diverge at the extremes. The averaged conservative range of 22–27°C is used (mid-range values from both sources); higher temperatures up to 28°C may be tolerated short-term but are not well-supported as target values across sources.

Nutrient tolerance: Unlike SPS corals, Actinodiscus generally tolerates slightly elevated nitrate (5–10 ppm) and phosphate (0.05–0.1 ppm). Ultra-low nutrient systems may cause browning. Moderate nutrients can support better coloration.

Reproduction: Reproduces readily in captivity via longitudinal fission (splitting), pedal laceration, and budding. Under good conditions, colonies have been reported to multiply rapidly. Fragging by cutting is a commonly practiced propagation method.

Handling: Handle with care — all corallimorpharians possess nematocysts. Gloves are generally recommended; wash hands thoroughly after contact.
breedingNotes: Reproduces asexually in captivity via longitudinal fission, pedal laceration, and occasional budding. Under stable conditions, specimens have been reported to multiply rapidly and colonize available substrate. Fragging by cutting mature polyps is a commonly practiced propagation method. Sexual reproduction occurs on natural reefs but is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral (multiple product listings)
primary.notes: Three separate Actinodiscus sp. product pages consulted (Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral, Spotted Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral, Purple Speckled Mushroom Coral, Mushroom Coral Spotted). All gave consistent parameters: temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, lighting Moderate, flow Low-to-Medium, placement Bottom-to-Middle, temperament Semi-Aggressive, care level Easy.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Discosoma Mushroom Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Confirmed beginner care level, Indo-Pacific origin, PAR 50–150 (75–150 for Discosoma specifically), flow low-to-moderate, pH 8.0–8.4, alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C). Temperature upper end (84°F/28.9°C) is higher than LiveAquaria's 78°F cap; conservative mid-range of 22–27°C adopted for the JSON range. ReefChasers confirms Actinodiscus spp. = Discosoma synonymy.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Discosoma
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only. Confirmed: Family Discosomidae, Order Corallimorpharia, ~11 recognized species, Actinodiscus de Blainville 1830 listed as accepted synonym of Discosoma Rüppell & Leuckart 1828. Not used for husbandry parameters.
